title: LNCP Phase-6 Next-Step Prompt Pool
description: Pool of suggested next steps for Phase-6 summary. Each prompt invites the writer toward a specific kind of exploration based on their analysis results.

selection_guidance.note: LLM selects based on full analysis context. Rule-based fallback uses trigger conditions.

VOLUME.description: Prompts for writers with small sample sizes

DENSITY.description: Prompts for writers with low structural event density

MODAL.description: Prompts related to epistemic/modal marker usage
MODAL.trigger_condition: high_intent_profile.coverage_rate < 0.3 OR dominant_category in specific set

VARIETY.description: Prompts for writers with high signature concentration

DEPTH.description: Prompts for writers who have completed multiple rounds

EXPLORATION.description: General exploratory prompts for any writer

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to write something longer to see deeper patterns emerge?
prompts.rationale_template: With {sentence_count} sentences, we're seeing initial shapes. A longer sample would reveal whether these patterns persist or evolve.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try a focused writing exercise—three paragraphs on a single topic?
prompts.rationale_template: Sustained writing on one subject often reveals structural habits that shorter samples miss.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to experiment with adding structural layers—parentheses, dashes, or asides?
prompts.rationale_template: Your writing moves cleanly without many structural markers. Adding layers could reveal new textures in your prose.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try a 'bracketed thoughts' exercise—writing where every sentence contains an aside?
prompts.rationale_template: This exercise pushes structural density to the extreme, helping you feel what heavy layering does to rhythm and meaning.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to explore writing with more certainty—or more tentativeness?
prompts.rationale_template: Your epistemic stance leans {stance_direction}. Trying the opposite could expand your expressive range.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to practice 'hedged declarations'—statements that are firm but leave room for doubt?
prompts.rationale_template: Balancing certainty and possibility is a subtle skill. This exercise helps you find the middle ground.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try writing without any stance markers—pure description, no opinion?
prompts.rationale_template: Your writing contains {marker_count} epistemic markers. Removing them entirely reveals what remains.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to experiment with different sentence shapes—varying your structural patterns?
prompts.rationale_template: Your top patterns account for {coverage}% of your sentences. Deliberate variation could add texture.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try a 'constraint' exercise—writing where no two sentences can have the same structure?
prompts.rationale_template: This exercise forces structural variety, which can feel awkward but reveals new possibilities.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to compare this sample to your previous one and see what shifted?
prompts.rationale_template: You've now completed {rounds} rounds. Comparing them reveals how your structural patterns vary across contexts.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try the same prompts again, writing differently on purpose?
prompts.rationale_template: Revisiting prompts with deliberate structural choices can deepen your awareness of how you build sentences.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try writing in a different register—formal, casual, or poetic?
prompts.rationale_template: Different registers often produce different structural patterns. This exercise reveals how context shapes form.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to write about the same topic in two different moods?
prompts.rationale_template: Mood shifts often surface in structural choices. Comparing the two versions can be illuminating.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to try 'structural imitation'—copying the shape of a sentence you admire?
prompts.rationale_template: Imitation is a powerful learning tool. By copying structure (not content), you internalize new patterns.

prompts.prompt_text: Would you like to save this analysis and return later to see how your writing evolves?
prompts.rationale_template: Structural patterns shift over time. Returning later gives you a longitudinal view of your writing.

forward_pointers.title: Extended Analysis
forward_pointers.description: Write a full page and receive deeper pattern analysis with cross-sentence insights.

forward_pointers.title: Structural Exercises
forward_pointers.description: Guided exercises that help you practice specific structural patterns.

forward_pointers.title: Comparison Mode
forward_pointers.description: Compare two samples side-by-side to see how your patterns vary.

forward_pointers.title: Register Exploration
forward_pointers.description: Explore how your structure changes across formal, casual, and creative registers.

forward_pointers.title: Longitudinal Tracking
forward_pointers.description: Track your structural patterns over weeks or months to see evolution.
